Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
VCTR 3.13 30.5 34.8 62.3 65.4
HWBK 2.75 12.9 11.4 13.3 18.5 16
NDAQ 1.29 9.1 10.1 8.9 17 10.2

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $68B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four main factors in the index that SCHD tracks.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

VCTR doesn't have a 10-year dividend growth rate, nor a 10-year public stock history, so I'm excluding them.

SCHD returned 176% over ten years, with dividends reinvested. Now, both companies that I'm comparing, HWBK and NDAQ, managed to outperform that benchmark.

I'm actually not surprised; they each have a 10-year dividend growth rate in the upper teens. That kind of compounding usually happens when earnings are growing strong. Stock prices also tend to follow high earnings growth.

NDAQ has been especially impressive with its total return. Both companies have had a history of outperforming SCHD to varying degrees.

Next Steps

I'm pretty picky when it comes to individual holdings in my portfolio. I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD, not just slightly beating it. Even from there, I want to see strong earnings and dividend growth over time. I also prefer to have an interest in the company, though not having a strong personal relationship isn't necessarily required (but it helps). These articles are my opportunity to review what has performed well and assess my current holdings as they provide their annual updates.

NDAQ has been a great performer, has been on my watch list for some time. That said, I hold 25 individual dividend growth stars, so the bar is set pretty high. At this point, it'll remain on my watch list, and I'll watch the story develop from the sidelines.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: SJB: Tactically Bullish As A Shady Credit Risk Environment Emerges
data.description:
Financial markets are in search of direction and the bond market is no different. Illustrating that claim is the fact that U.S. high-yield credit spreads have ranged between 259 and 461 basis points since the turn of the year. Moreover, real economic talking points surrounding both fiscal and monetary policy have seemingly struck fever pitch as many might argue that the "region of stability," has been breached.

Considering the current market climate, we think ProShares Short High Yield ETF (NYSEARCA:SJB) deserves coverage. As explained in the body of the analysis, we expect high-yield option-adjusted spreads to expand in late 2025 or early 2026, which, in turn places our outlook of SJB ETF in bullish territory.

Herewith is a deeper explanation of our outlook and why we think SJB ETF is well-aligned.

What Is SJB ETF?

The Fund's Optics

ProShares Short High Yield ETF seeks to -1x the daily return of the Markit iBoxx® $ Liquid High Yield Index, though longer-term returns may differ due to various technical reasons with transactional liquidity and basis risk being a few. Moreover, SJB ETF's returns are before-fee targeted, meaning investors will have to fork out a premium to gain exposure to the fund's managers.

The idea behind the concept is for investors to access a hedging tool when they think high-yield credit risk is unfavourable. This could be exceptionally valuable if you're unwilling to sell out of long-term positions. Furthermore, SJB ETF can be used as a short-selling mechanism for speculators or underwriters who'd like to offset their costs with fund-level distributions.

Strategically, SJB ETF uses derivatives to achieve its goals, whereby it engages in swap agreements. While not explicitly stated, we know these agreements as Total Return Swap Agreements, which is a lean way to engage in directional views, especially if you hold a collateral base of unencumbered securities. That said, unforeseen market events can lead to asymmetrical liquidity and higher collateral demands. Generally speaking, we're looking at a highly specialized strategy, substantiating SJB's gross expense ratio of 1%.

A view of SJB's total returns shows why it's generally suitable as a tactical ETF instead of a long-term position. Unlike its counterpart, HYG ETF (HYG), SJB has suffered negative returns since its inception, highlighting its used case interim exposure. Despite its negative long-run returns, SJB has shown sharp increases whenever credit spreads have dislocated (see succeeding chart).

Lastly, SJB has a distribution history. Although unsustainable, we think SJB's sparse distributions serve as a trading cost softener for retail investors.

The Index It's Shorting

SJB ETF shorts an index, which, according to S&P Global's (SPGI) material, is "designed to reflect the performance of USD denominated high yield corporate debt. The index rules aim to offer a broad coverage of the USD high yield liquid bond universe. The indices are an integral part of the global iBoxx index families, which provide the marketplace with accurate and objective indices by which to assess the performance of bond markets and investments. The index is market-value weighted with an issuer cap of 3%."

The fund's composition shows that it is sector diversified with a slight tilt towards the industrial and consumer services sectors. Furthermore, the index isolates the 1-to-10 year maturities, has a modified duration of 3.07, a weighted average coupon of 6.37%, and possesses broad issuer diversification, spanning around 1,240 issuers. In our view, we might see exposure adjustments throughout time, especially as economic cycle evolves. That being said, the index's weighting methodology means it will likely retain similar short-to-intermediate-term exposure.

Market Commentary

General Outlook For High-Yield Bonds

We are bearish credit risk, especially high-yield credit risk, for numerous reasons. Firstly, we deem the spread levels unattractive. High-yield spreads are below historical averages, which suggests docile excess return possibilities, likely introduced by investors chasing income for too long.

In essence, we deem the risk-return utility of high-yield bonds unappealing. In addition, we see spread widening occurring in due course, which we base on the following reasons.

  • Credit spreads can be mean-reverting – mean-reversion might be overdue.
  • The U.S.'s economic indicators are quite dicey and we expect these metrics to stay under pressure due to the U.S. arguably being in late-cycle, which has been perpetuated by an unstable fiscal policy environment. Moreover, geopolitical risks aren't abating, which adds pressure to global confidence levels.
Metric Current 1-Y Trailing
Quarterly U.S. GDP Growth -0.2% 1.6%
Leading Economic Index 99.4 101.7
Business Confidence 48.5 48.5
Consumer Confidence 52.5 69.1
Unemployment Rate 4.2% 3.9%
CapEx Index 27 20.1
10-Y CDS 48.54 circa 41.36 circa
PCE 2.1% 2.72%

Source: Trading Economics - Not All Metrics Are Measured In The Same Period, But All Are Y/Y Since Their Latest Recording.

  • Interest coverage ratios reached a bottom in early 2025, likely due to relief from interest rate cuts in late-2024. However, a loss in global economic confidence remains of concerns, especially for higher-risk borrowers. Moreover, we think tariffs will cut EBITDA margins of higher-risk companies that can't pass input cost rises to consumers. Collectively, we see fundamental headwinds emerging, especially in cyclical sectors.

A Model To Quantify Our Outlook

I built a model to quantify our views where I drew inspiration from Gilchrist and Zakrajšek, who, in 2012 delivered an option-adjusted spread pricing model, dictated by various economic activity factors and an anomalous factor. Our derivation didn't replicate the seminal model because Gilchrist and Zakrajšek performed numerous steps to prepare and fine tune the modelling process; instead, we merely drew inspiration from its pricing factors.

We used five variables, including GDP growth, inflation, fed funds rate, the yield curve's slope, and the VIX, to model high-yield OAS. Our model's output shows that it tracks quite closely to historical data with dislocations occurring from time-to-time. Our regression stopped on March 31st, pre-reciprocal tariff fiasco, communicating that option-adjusted spreads were likely underpriced at that stage (by about 100 basis points). Since then, we've arguably seen sustained economic risks, yet, the high-yield option-adjusted spread remains docile at around 319 basis points.

Side Note: The regressions smooths data to quarterly averages, meaning the OAS plotted below are interpolated and not point-in-time values.

The following charts supports the base regression by showing an adjusted R-squared of 68.3%, suggesting the model has strong explanatory abilities. Moreover, we used t-stats (explained here) to see which variables play a substantial role in pricing U.S. high-yield OAS. The VIX explains a lot but is difficult to predict. Furthermore, GDP growth, which is currently negative, explains a noticeable amount, and the yield curve's slope explains a statistically significant amount – remember, bullish yield steepening, usually suggests a recession has materialised, which is why it makes sense that the t-stat holds gravity in this instance.

The final piece of the model is the excess bond premium, which describes the portion of the OAS unexplained by default risk. The premium fluctuates quite substantially, yet negative values have been salient since the COVID-19 pandemic, suggesting default risk has dominated the spread in recent years, most likely due to fiscal dominance and unfamiliar monetary policies.

Kindly note the following limitations that pertain to the model:

  • Additional performance metrics need to be observed to paint a comprehensive picture of the model's accuracy.
  • The unexplained part of the model wasn't studied. The residual terms can form certain types of codependencies, biasing the explained part of the model's output.
  • Past relationships aren't necessarily perpetual. They might rhyme but not repeat.

How All This Plays Into SJB

You might've gathered by now that we expect option-adjusted spreads to move materially higher. In fact, we expect two things: 1) Spreads will move materially higher as we lead into the pronounced part of the credit cycle, and 2) occasional spikes due to the unstable political environment, led by narrative.

SJB is currently dislocated from HYG, where HYG ETF has drifted higher versus SJB since late 2023. Real economic growth has stayed solid during the earlier-stages of the dislocation period (and inflation cooled), likely justifying the move. However, we think it's time for convergence, where a change in real economic and market-based variables will lead the two ETF's closer.

Recent history shows three spikes in high-yield option-adjusted spreads during the past decade. If we reference B-rated OAS, a spike to around 650 basis points occurred in 2022 amid a stock bear market and bankin stability concerns; in 2020, we saw a spike above the 1100 basis point level when a structural shock occurred; and in 2015/2016, a level of circa 900 unfolded against the backdrop of global demand concerns. Correspondingly, I calculated that SJB's price rose by around 15.1%, 24.86%, and 10.33% during those periods (note: subject to rounding errors) – We believe we'll see a similar result materialise in late 2025 or early-2026.

Risk Factors (Very Important To Note)

Here are a few risk factors worth considering.

Firstly, SJB ETF can be exceptionally risky. Not just because of its negative return history but also because of its substantial max drawdown. Lower standard deviation is evident but standard deviation doesn't communicate the full impact of tail events such as stressed market circumstances.

Secondly, SJB faces a fundamental headwind: U.S.-based corporates are upping their reverse yankee issuances. In simple terms, U.S. companies have spotted issuance opportunities outside the U.S., particularly in Europe. As a consequence, we could see lower U.S. high-yield supply in the coming years, limiting the downside possibilities of high-yield bonds due to supply/demand imbalances.

Finally, coupons are quite high on the underlying index. The Markit iBoxx® $ Liquid High Yield Index has a weighted average coupon of above 6%, adding a cushion for those with long positions in total return swaps. To our understanding, short TRS' will require substantial price downside to combat the coupon effect, at least for now.

Conclusion

We think ProShares Short High Yield ETF provides a valuable tool for hedging a portfolio's short-term credit risk and/or shorting high-risk credit. Characteristically, SJB provides broad-based exposure and low modified duration, isolating the effect of systematic variables such as option-adjusted spreads and sector fundamentals.

We used a seminal research model and headline economic indicators to conclude that high-yield option-adjusted spreads were materially underpriced at the close of March. Since then, economic risk has arguably intensified, yet, spreads have yet to catch-up. Moreover, we believe interest coverage ratios will inevitably reflect the impact of tariffs and a potential loss of business confidence.

While factors such as an increase in reverse yankee issuances and bulky high-yield coupons can diminish SJB's interim outlook, we are net bullish, and have therefore decided to communicate our bullish outlook on SJB ETF with a horizon of 6-to-12 months.
